core_intuitions: The reward model is a proxy that is accurate only LOCALLY, near the reference distribution it was trained on; optimizing it hard pushes the policy off-distribution, where the RM extrapolates arbitrarily and proxy reward decouples from true reward.

Extremal Goodhart specifically: once the policy leaves the RM's training SUPPORT, the neural net has no signal there and its output is essentially arbitrary (often spuriously high) — this is the mechanistic root of hacking.

The KL-reward frontier: as KL from the reference grows, proxy reward rises monotonically while TRUE reward peaks at a moderate KL and then falls; the optimum lives at the peak, not at zero KL and not at max optimization.

The real-world failure taxonomy (sycophancy, length bias, spurious format rewards, specification gaming) are all instances of the policy satisfying the LETTER of r_theta while violating the SPIRIT r*.

Mitigations attack the gap from two sides: keep the policy IN-distribution (adaptive KL, online RM updates re-train the RM on current-policy data) or make the reward robust to the gap (ensemble disagreement as an uncertainty penalty, verifiable rewards).

Ensemble reward models turn DISAGREEMENT into a usable signal: RMs agree where they share data and fan out off-distribution, so a variance penalty automatically deflates exactly the spurious off-distribution peaks.

existing.name: rewardhack-kl-frontier
existing.issue: The frontier shape (proxy monotone up, true peaks then falls, shared KL divider) is well conceived, but the render breaks in dark theme and has glyph/clipping defects. In dark mode several labels are near-black on the dark panel and effectively invisible: the left 'r_theta' axis label, the 'Proxy reward r_theta' panel title, 'gap ~ dKL', and 'r* falls; r_theta still rises'. Multiple unicode glyphs render as tombstone boxes ('rate ~ [box]KL' where a radical was intended, 'beta [box][box]' arrows, subscripts). The bottom caption line is clipped at the viewBox edge in the dark render. Two labels collide with the data: 'reward hacking regime' overlaps the endpoint dot and 'peak (optimal) beta' overlaps the true-reward curve.
existing.improvement: Route ALL text through a theme-aware foreground color (currentColor / CSS var), never a fixed dark ink, so every label is legible on both light and dark panels. Replace every unicode box glyph with safe equivalents: write 'sqrt(KL)' instead of a radical, spell arrows as 'up' or use a drawn triangle, and render subscripts as '_theta'/'_ref' plain text. Add bottom padding (raise viewBox height / lower the plot) so the caption line is fully inside the frame. Nudge 'reward hacking regime' up-left off its dot and float 'peak (optimal) beta' above the curve with a short leader so neither overlaps the plotted line. Consider dropping the redundant left panel's separate title styling so the two panels read as one comparison.

existing.name: rewardhack-online-rm-loop
existing.issue: The two-loop structure is genuinely good: a fast inner loop (generate -> score -> policy update) and a slow every-N-steps outer loop (sample pairs -> annotate -> fine-tune RM -> replace RM) with a green 'updated RM re-enters inner loop' path. In light mode it reads cleanly. But in dark mode the BODY text inside the blue/orange bordered, transparent-fill nodes is dark and low-contrast: 'y ~ pi_t(.|x) [policy pi_t]', 'r_theta(x,y) [reward model]', 'pi_t -> pi_{t+1} [PPO step]', 'theta -> theta prime' are hard to read against the dark background, while the D-node white sub-boxes read fine. The left-margin annotation boxes are clipped ('RM stays in-distribution...' shows as 'M stays...'), losing the leading letter.
existing.improvement: Give every in-node body line a theme-aware text fill (foreground color, or add a subtle theme-aware panel behind the text as the D-nodes already have) so all node contents are legible in dark mode. Shift the left-margin annotation boxes right / add left padding so 'RM stays in-distribution with drifting policy pi_t' and the 'automated variant' box are fully inside the viewBox. Keep the inner/outer loop layout and the green re-entry path unchanged — that is the load-bearing intuition and it works.

new_figures.name: rewardhack-rm-extrapolation
new_figures.concept: Why proxy and true reward diverge: the RM fits the true reward only where it has training data near pi_ref, and extrapolates upward into a spurious peak off-distribution while true quality actually collapses there.
new_figures.rationale: This is THE mechanistic root of the whole chapter and it is currently undrawn. The existing frontier figure shows the CONSEQUENCE (curves of reward vs KL); this shows the CAUSE (a reward landscape overfit near pi_ref, extrapolating arbitrarily where there is no data). It makes 'extremal Goodhart', 'off-distribution', and 'proxy >> true' click as one geometric picture, and every mitigation later in the chapter is legible as an attempt to close or avoid this exact gap.
new_figures.placement: Goodhart's Law and Why It Bites LLMs (immediately after the r_theta >> r* off-distribution equation, before the four-modes table)
new_figures.brief: One wide 2D plot. X-axis is a schematic 1D slice through response/output space, labeled 'output space (schematic): distance from pi_ref'; tick a marker 'pi_ref' at the left-center. Y-axis is 'reward'. Draw two regions separated by a soft vertical fade at roughly 45-55% width: LEFT = 'RM training support' (lightly shaded, theme-aware), RIGHT = 'off-distribution: no RM data'. Plot a cluster of small dots = 'RM preference data' sitting in the left region ON the true-reward curve. TRUE reward r* (theme accent A, solid): a gentle hump that rises modestly through the data region then DECLINES in the right region (degenerate/gibberish outputs are genuinely poor). PROXY reward r_theta (theme accent B): coincides with r* through the data region (draw them overlapping where the dots are), but at the support boundary it KEEPS RISING, breaking away into a tall spurious peak in the right region; render that off-support continuation as a dashed/uncertain stroke to signal 'no data, arbitrary extrapolation'. Place a policy marker as a dot starting near pi_ref with a bold arrow labeled 'optimization pressure (KL grows)' pointing right toward the spurious proxy peak; annotate the peak 'policy climbs proxy peak'. Directly below that peak, drop a vertical bracket from the high proxy curve down to the low true curve labeled 'r_theta >> r*: hidden quality collapse'. In the off-distribution region add a small tag 'extremal Goodhart: RM extrapolates'. Caption along the bottom: 'The reward model is accurate only where it has data. Push the policy past that support and proxy reward keeps climbing while true reward falls.' Theme-safe: no hardcoded ink for text (use foreground), only two curve accents, no invented numbers — the only axis annotation is 'pi_ref'. Everything inside the viewBox; keep the two curves and the bracket from overlapping labels.

new_figures.name: rewardhack-ensemble-uncertainty
new_figures.concept: Reward-model ensembles turn disagreement into an uncertainty penalty: the RMs agree where they share training data and fan out off-distribution, so subtracting a variance penalty automatically deflates the spurious off-distribution peaks.
new_figures.rationale: The ensemble + variance-penalty mitigation is one of the chapter's most important defenses and is described only in equations and code. A single geometric frame makes it intuitive WHY disagreement is a free extremal-hacking detector: it directly reuses the extrapolation picture from the priority-1 figure (tight bundle near pi_ref, wide fan-out off-distribution) and shows the penalized reward collapsing exactly where variance balloons. This closes the loop between 'why the gap opens' and 'how one mitigation blunts it'.
new_figures.placement: Mitigations: KL Control, Reward Ensembles, and Online RM Updates -> Reward Ensembles (after the ensemble-mean and variance equations)
new_figures.brief: Reuse the same horizontal axis as rewardhack-rm-extrapolation: X = 'output space: distance from pi_ref' with a 'pi_ref' marker at left and a soft fade at mid-width separating 'RM training support' (left) from 'off-distribution' (right). Plot K = 3 or 4 thin individual reward curves (theme-neutral, distinguished by opacity/linestyle, NOT by hardcoded colors), all bundled TIGHTLY together through the left data region (they agree where they share data), then FANNING OUT widely in the right region because each RM extrapolates differently. Overlay a shaded 'disagreement band' = variance/std across the curves: thin (near-zero) on the left, ballooning on the right. Draw the ensemble MEAN reward as one bold curve (accent A) — it still shows a spurious hump on the right. Then draw the PENALIZED reward = mean - lambda*std as a second bold curve (accent B) that tracks the mean on the left but is pulled sharply DOWN in the right region, flattening/killing the spurious off-distribution peak. Annotate: near pi_ref 'RMs agree -> penalty ~ 0'; in the fan-out region 'RMs disagree -> large variance penalty'; at the deflated peak 'spurious off-distribution peak suppressed'. Bottom caption: 'Where the ensemble fans out, the variance penalty deflates reward — the policy is discouraged from chasing peaks the RMs cannot agree on.' Theme-safe foreground text, only two bold accent curves plus neutral thin members and a neutral variance band, no invented numbers, all content inside the viewBox with no label/curve overlaps.

summary: Chapter is concept-rich with two solid but dark-theme-broken figures. Both existing figures earn REFINE (not replace): the KL-frontier plot is well designed but its labels go near-invisible in dark mode, several unicode glyphs render as tombstones (sqrt, arrows, subscripts), the caption clips, and two labels collide with the curves; the online-RM-loop has excellent inner/outer loop structure but its in-node body text is illegible in dark mode and its left annotations clip. Both fixes are the same class: theme-aware foreground text and viewBox padding. The chapter's single deepest intuition — WHY proxy and true reward diverge (the RM is accurate only within its training support and extrapolates arbitrarily off-distribution) — is undrawn; the frontier figure shows the consequence, not the cause. New priority-1 figure 'rewardhack-rm-extrapolation' supplies that reward-landscape picture, and priority-2 'rewardhack-ensemble-uncertainty' reuses the same axis to make the ensemble variance-penalty mitigation click. Both new figures are static single frames; no animation needed.
